senses.definition: A strip or ridge of land, especially behind houses, mainly in parts of northern England and Scotland.
examples: Their cottage backed onto a narrow rigg used for growing vegetables.
Old maps show several riggs stretching out behind the main street.

senses.definition: A family name or given name that comes from an Old English word meaning someone who lives on a ridge.
examples: Mr Rigg grew up in a small village in Yorkshire.
They named their son Rigg after his grandfather.

senses.definition: Arranged in a dishonest or unfair way so that the result is controlled.
examples: Some people claimed that the election was rigged.
The competition felt rigged in favour of the bigger companies.

senses.definition: A worker who puts up and adjusts ropes, cables, or equipment, especially on ships, buildings, or in film and theatre work.
examples: A team of riggers set up the lights above the stage.
The shipyard hired more riggers to work on the new vessel.

senses.definition: Plural of rigger: workers who put up and adjust ropes, cables, or technical equipment.
examples: The riggers arrived early to prepare the concert venue.
Film riggers spent hours setting up the camera rig.

senses.definition: The system of ropes, wires, and chains used to support and control the sails on a ship or equipment on a structure.
examples: The sailors checked the rigging before leaving the harbour.
Loose rigging on the tower had to be tightened.

senses.definition: The act of arranging something in a dishonest way to control the result, especially in elections or markets.
examples: There was a public inquiry into the rigging of prices.
Voters protested against the alleged rigging of the election.

senses.definition: Different sets or parts of rigging, especially on ships or structures with many ropes or cables.
examples: The storm damaged the ship's masts and riggings.
They inspected all the riggings before the climb.

senses.definition: Wanton, sexually playful, or behaving in a bold way that is not considered proper, used in older English.
examples: The heroine is sometimes described as a riggish young woman.
His letters complain about the riggish manners of the city girls.

senses.definition: A playful or old-fashioned term for a person who plays tricks or jokes on others.
examples: He was known as a bit of a riggite in his youth.
The children, little riggites that they were, hid their teacher's bag.

senses.definition: Surface drainage, especially rainwater running off an area, in some English dialects.
examples: After the storm, the riggot carved small channels in the path.
The field shows marks of old riggots after heavy rain.

senses.definition: Plural of rigg: strips or ridges of land, especially behind houses or in fields, mainly in northern English and Scottish use.
examples: Old photos show narrow riggs stretching out behind the cottages.
Farmers once worked these riggs by hand.

senses.definition: Plural form of the family name Rigg, used to refer to that family.
examples: The Riggs moved to the village in the 19th century.
Local records list several Riggs among the early settlers.

senses.definition: True, correct, or not wrong.
examples: You are right; the answer is twelve.
Check that you have the right phone number.

senses.definition: Morally good, fair, or acceptable.
examples: It is not right to lie to your friends.
They tried to do the right thing in a difficult situation.

senses.definition: On or towards the side of your body where most people have their writing hand.
examples: She hurt her right hand in the game.
Turn right at the next traffic light.

senses.definition: Something that you are allowed to do or have by law, by rules, or by moral principles.
examples: Everyone has the right to speak freely.
They fought for equal rights for all citizens.

senses.definition: Exactly or completely, or immediately.
examples: The station is right in the city centre.
Please come right away.

senses.definition: To put something back into the correct position, or to correct a wrong situation.
examples: They tried to right the boat after it turned over.
The company is working hard to right past mistakes.

senses.definition: Possible to correct or put right.
examples: The damage is serious but still rightable.
Some social injustices are rightable if people act together.

senses.definition: The quality of being morally right or just.
examples: He never questioned the rightableness of their cause.
They debated the rightableness of strict new laws.

senses.definition: Plural of rightableness: different kinds or examples of the quality of being morally right.
examples: The article examines various rightablenesses claimed by different groups.
They argued over the competing rightablenesses involved in the conflict.

senses.definition: In a way that can be corrected or put right, or in a morally right way.
examples: The situation is rightably changed through legal reform.
Such problems are rightably addressed by the community.

senses.definition: A military movement where a person or group turns to face the opposite direction.
examples: At the command 'rightabout', the soldiers turned sharply.
He stepped forward, then did a rightabout and marched back.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of right: put something back into the correct position or correct a wrong situation.
examples: They righted the boat after it capsized.
The new policy righted many past injustices.

senses.definition: To make something right or more correct; to improve or repair something that is wrong, mainly in older or dialect use.
examples: The reforms were meant to righten the unfair system.
They tried to righten relations between the two countries.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of righten: made something right or more correct, mainly in older or dialect use.
examples: Old laws were finally rightened by the new government.
Relations between the neighbours were rightened over time.

senses.definition: The process of making something right or more correct, especially in moral or social matters, mainly in older or dialect use.
examples: The book describes the gradual rightening of society after the crisis.
They hoped for a rightening of past wrongs.

senses.definition: Third person singular of righten: makes something right or more correct, mainly in older or dialect use.
examples: Time rightens many small injustices.
A fair legal system rightens wrongs.

senses.definition: Morally good, honest, and fair.
examples: She is known as a righteous judge.
They believed they were fighting a righteous war.

senses.definition: Strongly believing that your ideas or actions are morally right, sometimes in an annoying way.
examples: He answered with a righteous certainty that upset others.
Her righteous tone made discussion difficult.

senses.definition: In a morally good or fair way.
examples: They tried to act righteously in difficult times.
He believes that power should be used righteously.

senses.definition: The quality of being morally good and fair.
examples: She was admired for her righteousness.
The story explores themes of sin and righteousness.

senses.definition: The belief that you or your group are morally right, especially when this is too strong.
examples: There was a note of righteousness in his criticism.
Political debates can be full of moral righteousness.

senses.definition: Plural of righteousness: different types or examples of moral goodness.
examples: The text compares the different righteousnesses of the characters.
They spoke of the various righteousnesses demanded by their faith.

senses.definition: More correct or more morally good than something else.
examples: Of the two plans, hers seems the righter choice.
He looked for the righter path, not the easier one.

senses.definition: A person who tries to correct wrongs or injustices.
examples: He saw himself as a righter of social wrongs.
History remembers her as a brave righter of injustice.

senses.definition: People who try to correct wrongs or injustices.
examples: Many social movements are led by passionate righters of wrongs.
Writers and artists can be powerful righters in society.

senses.definition: Most correct or most morally good.
examples: It may not be the easiest solution, but it feels the rightest.
Choosing honesty was the rightest decision he ever made.

senses.definition: Directly forward or straight out, used in older or literary English.
examples: He strode rightforth into the dark hall.
The river runs rightforth to the sea.

senses.definition: Having a fair or legal claim to something.
examples: She is the rightful owner of the house.
He finally took his rightful place as leader.

senses.definition: In a way that is fair, legal, or morally correct.
examples: The prize was rightfully hers.
They were rightfully proud of their achievements.

senses.definition: The state of being fair, legal, or morally correct.
examples: They questioned the rightfulness of the new tax.
Her speech defended the rightfulness of their claim.

senses.definition: Plural of rightfulness: different kinds or examples of being fair, legal, or morally correct.
examples: The text explores the competing rightfulnesses of both sides.
Different legal systems claim different rightfulnesses.

senses.definition: On or towards the right side.
examples: Take the first righthand turn after the bridge.
The righthand column shows last year's results.

senses.definition: Very important or trusted, like a main helper.
examples: She is the manager's righthand assistant.
He became the CEO's righthand man.

senses.definition: Thinking in a sensible and morally good way.
examples: Her rightheaded approach impressed the committee.
We need some rightheaded people to lead this project.

senses.definition: Kind and having good intentions.
examples: She is a righthearted woman who helps her neighbours.
His righthearted offer came at the perfect time.

senses.definition: More right, or more morally acceptable, than something else.
examples: Apologizing seems the rightier option here.
Of the two, this is the rightier course of action.

senses.definition: People who write with their right hand.
examples: The class had more righties than lefties.
Righties often find it easier to use these scissors.

senses.definition: People or groups with right‑wing political views.
examples: The righties and lefties argued all night about policy.
Some righties opposed the new tax plan.

senses.definition: Most right or most morally acceptable.
examples: Telling the truth is the rightiest choice.
We are trying to find the rightiest solution for everyone.

senses.definition: The act of putting something back into the correct position or correcting a wrong situation.
examples: The righting of the boat took several minutes.
The new law is part of the righting of past injustices.

senses.definition: Plural of righting: different acts of putting things right.
examples: History is full of rightings and new injustices.
They celebrated the rightings that had taken place over the last decade.

senses.definition: Close to right or somewhat correct.
examples: Your estimate is rightish, but not exact.
He gave a rightish answer without knowing all the facts.

senses.definition: Having somewhat right‑wing political views.
examples: Her views are rightish but not extreme.
A rightish party won the election.

senses.definition: Right‑wing political beliefs or support for conservative ideas.
examples: The party has moved towards rightism in recent years.
His essays criticise both leftism and rightism.

senses.definition: Plural of rightism: different forms or examples of right‑wing political beliefs.
examples: The book compares various rightisms across Europe.
Debates focused on the new rightisms emerging in society.

senses.definition: A person who has right‑wing political opinions.
examples: Rightists and leftists disagreed on almost everything.
The new policy was welcomed by many rightists.

senses.definition: Having or relating to right‑wing political views.
examples: He joined a rightist organisation in his youth.
The government adopted several rightist policies.

senses.definition: People who have right‑wing political opinions.
examples: Rightists and leftists clashed during the protest.
Many rightists supported the tax cuts.

senses.definition: To set something in order or put it right, mainly in dialect or older use.
examples: Her husband's been helping her to get things rightled a bit.
They worked hard to rightle the tangled accounts.
